All 5 Great Lakes are currently record-warm, from about +2°C (+3.6°F) in Lake Superior to +3.6°C (6.5°F) in Lake Michigan. Over the past 50 years, summertime water temperatures have warmed by 3°C (5°F) in Lake Superior and Huron, and 1.7°C (3°F) in Lake Michigan and Erie.

One of the last freighters sailing on Lake Superior in 2025, MV Paul R. Tregurtha, the current Queen of the Lakes, passed Grand Marais on December 29th. The Queen of the Lakes designation recognizes the Paul R. Tregurtha, at 1,013.5 feet, as the largest ship of the Great Lakes fleet.

No, this is not the ice planet Hoth. This is The shoreline of Lake Superior in Michigan two days ago with -10° temps, gale Force winds of over 40 mph, and gusts over 50 mph. In these conditions frostbite happens in minutes. Hard to imagine the incredible strength of indigenous people to live here.

On this day in 1902, the SS Bannockburn was sighted by the captain of the SS Algonquin on Lake Superior amid a snowstorm. The ship was never seen again. The wreck of the ship has never been located. Only one piece of wreckage, a cork life preserver, was found weeks later.

The SS Meteor (1896) is the worlds only surviving "Whaleback" ship. The whaleback were a special kind of ships built in the late nineteenth hundreds, designed to carry mostly grain and ore on the Great Lakes. Later refitted as a tanker the ship was finally made into a museum ship in 1971.

Fresh Water is one of the reasons the Great Lakes punch above their weight in shipwrecks. Less buoyancy, more frequent and steeper waves, and slop instead of rollers. They can come from multiple directions. Because of the cold temperatures in lake superior bodies don't bloat & float. They remain.

Most importantly though, what you need to know is:
1) Between 1875 and 1975 the number of Great Lakes shipwrecks amounted to ONE SHIP PER WEEK FOR AN ENTIRE CENTURY.
2) There has not been a ship lost to the Great Lakes since November 10, 1975.

One is on the great lakes and lacks capacity for Arctic conditions.
The second one is somewhat more capable, but still lack true Arctic capacity and had to be assisted during its jaunts up North into the Arctic.
Assisted by EU ships no less.

Everyone ignore me, I have to get really pedantic about the great lakes for a sec. That pic I just reskeeted is amazing, but whitecaps aren't when they're on the shore. Those are just waves. Whitecaps are when waves peak far out from shore and it means the lake is rougher than usual.
